sections.title: Stakeholder feedback on outsourcing guidelines scope
sections.summary: This section summarizes stakeholder feedback on the scope of the EBA's outsourcing guidelines, noting that while many respondents acknowledged the EBA's efforts, they suggested reducing the regulatory burden by narrowing the scope of the guidelines.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines stakeholder concerns about the scope of outsourcing guidelines, indicating potential for reduced regulatory burden in specific contexts.

sections.summary: Respondents requested clarification that the guidelines do not apply to subsidiaries not subject to CRD on a solo basis, particularly alternative investment funds, UCITS, and third-country subsidiaries. They also sought a non-exhaustive list of arrangements that should not be considered outsourcing under the guidelines.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses scope limitations for outsourcing arrangements, indicating that DTIAs should exclude non-critical financial entities and define what constitutes outsourcing to avoid overreach.

sections.title: Lighter requirements for intragroup outsourcing
sections.summary: Respondents requested lighter requirements for intragroup outsourcing, institutional protection schemes, and cooperative networks due to lower operational risks, including reduced governance, conflict of interest, and monitoring obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section indicates that DTIAs should apply reduced requirements for intragroup outsourcing arrangements where common service providers minimize risks.

requirements.text: Lighter governance requirements for intragroup outsourcing
Reduced conflict of interest obligations
Simplified pre-outsourcing analysis
Less frequent monitoring and audit requirements
Streamlined exit rights provisions
Reduced business continuity planning obligations
Simplified documentation and notification requirements
Less frequent compliance and reporting obligations

sections.source_quote: Respondents felt that intragroup outsourcing, outsourcing within institutional protection schemes and cooperative networks, and the inherent lower level of operational risks due to the use of common service providers were not sufficiently considered; respondents suggested that lighter requirements be applied in relation to several matters (e.g. governance requirements, conflicts of interest, pre-outsourcing analysis, monitoring and audit requirements, exit rights, business continuity planning, documentation and notification, compliance and reporting obligations).

sections.title: Principle-based contractual approach for outsourcing
sections.summary: Respondents found contractual requirements too demanding and specific, advocating for a more principle-based approach to reduce legal and practical challenges, particularly around audit rights and sub-outsourcing triggers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section indicates that DTIAs should prioritize principle-based contractual terms over detailed requirements to avoid excessive documentation and monitoring burdens.

requirements.text: Adopt a more principle-based approach to contractual requirements
Avoid including audit and access rights in contracts
Clarify sub-outsourcing triggers to prevent additional documentation and monitoring

sections.source_quote: Respondents found the contractual requirements too demanding and specific. They requested that a more principle-based approach be adopted and pointed out that certain expectations would raise significant legal and practical challenges, e.g. the inclusion of audit and access rights and the approach to sub-outsourcing that would trigger additional documentation and monitoring burdens.
sections.relevance_reason: Principle-based outsourcing for financial data compliance

sections.heading: Functions that are considered critical under a resolution perspective may also be outsourced.
sections.title: Cloud services as outsourcing: classification based on activity nature
sections.summary: This section states that cloud services should be treated as outsourcing following the same principles as other services. The classification of cloud services as important or critical functions depends on the nature of the activities being outsourced. Other respondents recommended maintaining specific guidance for cloud service providers.
sections.dtia_summary: Cloud services must be assessed for criticality based on activity nature, impacting how data transfers are evaluated in cross-border outsourcing scenarios.

requirements.text: Cloud services should be treated as outsourcing following the same principles as other services
Classification of cloud services as important or critical functions depends on the nature of the activities outsourced

sections.source_quote: Respondents suggested that the consideration of cloud services as outsourcing should follow the same principles as other services and determining whether they should be qualified as important or critical functions should depend on the nature of the activities outsourced.
sections.relevance_reason: Cloud outsourcing classification critical for GDPR compliance

sections.title: Outsourcing information to competent authorities: reduced burden and ex post notification
sections.summary: This section indicates that respondents found current requirements for information to competent authorities burdensome and with limited supervisory usefulness. They requested removal or conversion of the notification requirement for new critical or important outsourcing arrangements into an ex post notification, and that the register of outsourcing arrangements be limited to critical or important functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing notifications must be ex post rather than prior authorisation, and only critical functions require registration, reducing cross-border data transfer complexity.

requirements.text: Outsourcing arrangements documentation should not be limited to critical and important functions
New planned critical or important outsourcing arrangements must be notified to competent authorities ex post
The register of outsourcing arrangements should be limited to critical or important functions

sections.source_quote: Respondents considered that the requirements were burdensome and sometimes had limited supervisory usefulness; this concerns the documentation of all, and not only critical and important, outsourcing arrangements and the need to notify competent authorities of new planned critical or important outsourcing arrangements.
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing compliance critical for GDPR data transfers

sections.title: Differentiation between critical and non-critical outsourcing requirements
sections.summary: This section states that the guidelines have been reviewed to provide better differentiation between requirements for outsourcing critical and important functions (which require a stricter framework) and non-material outsourcing activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical functions require stricter cross-border data transfer safeguards than non-critical functions, influencing DTIA scope.

requirements.text: Outsourcing of critical and important functions requires a stricter framework
Outsourcing of non-material functions follows proportionate requirements

sections.source_quote: Overall, the guidelines have been reviewed to provide better differentiation between the requirements for the outsourcing of critical and important functions, to which a stricter framework applies, and for other, non-material, outsourcing.
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing critical functions under GDPR

sections.heading: For payment institutions, these guidelines should be read in conjunction with the EBA Guidelines
sections.title: MiFID II-aligned outsourcing framework structure
sections.summary: This section states that the guidelines have been restructured to mirror the MiFID II approach by defining outsourcing, critical and important functions, and setting out requirements for institutions when outsourcing such functions. Non-critical or non-important functions retain proportionate requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Clear definitions of outsourcing and critical functions enable consistent cross-border data transfer assessments under the MiFID II framework.

requirements.text: Outsourcing must be defined
Critical and important functions must be defined
Requirements for institutions when outsourcing critical functions must be set

sections.source_quote: The guidelines have been restructured to better mirror the MiFID II approach that (1) defines outsourcing, (2) defines critical and important functions and (3) sets out the requirements for institutions when outsourcing such functions.
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing framework structure for critical functions

sections.title: Consistency with EBA cloud recommendations and PSD2

sections.title: Cloud outsourcing requirements differentiation
sections.summary: This section states that the EBA retained all content from previous recommendations on cloud service provider outsourcing but adjusted the approach to align with the overall outsourcing framework. It differentiates requirements between critical/important outsourcing and non-material outsourcing arrangements to ensure consistent application across all outsourcing contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must differentiate between critical/important and non-material cloud outsourcing arrangements to determine appropriate safeguards and compliance requirements.

requirements.text: All content of previous recommendation on cloud service provider outsourcing retained
Changes made to ensure consistency with overall outsourcing framework
Differentiate requirements for critical/important outsourcing from non-material outsourcing

sections.source_quote: It should be noted that, in general, all of the content of the recommendation on outsourcing to cloud service providers has been retained; however, changes to the approach have been made to ensure consistency with the overall outsourcing framework, which differentiates between the requirements for critical and important outsourcing and those for other, non-material, outsourcing arrangements.

sections.title: Group and institutional protection scheme application
sections.summary: This section clarifies that while group and institutional protection schemes require special consideration, all institutions remain responsible for compliance with regulatory requirements. It emphasizes that institutions must apply the requirements in their specific context without transferring responsibility to other entities.
sections.dtia_summary: DTIAs must ensure that institutions maintain responsibility for compliance when applying outsourcing requirements across group structures and institutional protection schemes.

requirements.text: Group and institutional protection scheme contexts require special consideration
All institutions remain responsible for compliance with regulatory requirements

sections.source_quote: The application of the requirements in the context of a group and an institutional protection scheme have been clarified. While the context of groups and institutional protection schemes needs to be taken into account, all institutions remain responsible for compliance with regulatory requirements.

sections.title: Proportionality in compliance with outsourcing guidelines
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must apply the proportionality principle when complying with outsourcing guidelines. This principle ensures governance arrangements match the institution's risk profile, business model, and activity scale to effectively meet regulatory objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires proportionality in DTIA to ensure governance aligns with institutional risk, affecting outsourcing decisions.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should apply the proportionality principle when complying with outsourcing guidelines

sections.source_quote: 18. Institutions, payment institutions and competent authorities should, when complying or supervising compliance with these guidelines, have regard to the principle of proportionality.

sections.title: Factors for applying outsourcing requirements
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must consider outsourcing function complexity, associated risks, criticality/importance of functions, and potential impact on business continuity when applying outsourcing guidelines. This requirement applies to institutions and payment institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies factors to assess in DTIA for outsourcing, including risk and continuity impacts.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should consider complexity of outsourced functions
Institutions and payment institutions should consider risks arising from outsourcing arrangements
Institutions and payment institutions should consider criticality or importance of outsourced functions
Institutions and payment institutions should consider potential impact of outsourcing on business continuity

sections.source_quote: 19. When applying the requirements set out in these guidelines, institutions and payment institutions should take into account the complexity of the outsourced functions, the risks arising from the outsourcing arrangement, the criticality or importance of the outsourced function and the potential impact of the outsourcing on the continuity of their activities.

sections.title: Proportionality considerations for outsourcing governance
sections.summary: When applying proportionality, institutions and payment institutions must consider criteria from Title I of the EBA Guidelines on internal governance as specified in Article 74(2) of Directive 2013/36/EU. Competent authorities should ensure these criteria are met to balance regulatory requirements with operational needs. This applies to institutions and payment institutions under the EBA's oversight framework.

sections.title: Consolidation requirements for outsourcing governance
sections.summary: Outsourcing arrangements must be consistent and well-integrated across subsidiaries and payment institutions within institutional protection schemes. Parent undertakings must ensure internal governance mechanisms are adequate for effective guideline application at all levels, including critical functions. This applies to consolidated entities under EU prudential regulation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that parent entities maintain oversight of outsourcing arrangements across subsidiaries, affecting data transfer and processing when third parties are involved in cross-border operations.

sections.title: Group-level outsourcing compliance responsibilities
sections.summary: Institutions must retain full responsibility for compliance with regulatory requirements when outsourcing within groups or institutional protection schemes. They must ensure operational tasks for internal control functions are effectively performed through appropriate reporting mechanisms. This applies to institutions and payment institutions under group oversight frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to maintain oversight of outsourcing arrangements within groups, impacting data transfer and processing when third parties are involved across borders.

requirements.text: Management body retains full responsibility for compliance with all regulatory requirements
Institutions must ensure operational tasks for internal control functions are effectively performed

sections.source_quote: 22. Institutions and payment institutions, in accordance with paragraph 21, and institutions that, as members of an institutional protection scheme, use centrally provided governance arrangements should comply with the following: a. where those institutions or payment institutions have outsourcing arrangements with service providers within the group or the institutional protection scheme, the management body of those institutions or payment institutions retains, also for these outsourcing arrangements, full responsibility for compliance with all regulatory requirements and the effective application of these guidelines;
sections.relevance_reason: Group outsourcing compliance responsibilities under GDPR

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions must determine whether the function to be outsourced is
sections.title: Centralized outsourcing monitoring requirements
sections.summary: Institutions must ensure independent monitoring of service providers and oversight by each entity for critical functions. They must receive annual reports on risk assessments and performance monitoring, as well as audit reports. Institutions must also be informed of planned changes to service providers and their potential impacts on critical functions. This applies to centralized outsourcing arrangements within groups or institutional protection schemes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed oversight of centralized outsourcing arrangements, affecting data transfer and processing when third parties are involved across borders.

requirements.text: Institutions should ensure independent monitoring of service providers and oversight by each institution
Institutions must receive annual reports on risk assessments and performance monitoring
Institutions must receive summaries of audit reports for critical or important outsourcing arrangements
Institutions must be informed of planned changes to service providers and potential impacts on critical functions

sections.source_quote: 23. In addition to paragraph 22, institutions and payment institutions within a group for which no waivers have been granted on the basis of Article 109 of Directive 2013/36/EU and Article 7 of Regulation (EU) No 575/2013, institutions that are a central body or that are permanently affiliated to a central body for which no waivers have been granted on the basis of Article 21 of Directive 2013/36/EU, or institutions that are members of an institutional protection scheme should take into account the following: a. where the operational monitoring of outsourcing is centralised (e.g. as part of a master agreement for the monitoring of outsourcing arrangements), institutions and payment institutions should ensure that, at least for outsourced critical or important functions, both independent monitoring of the service provider and appropriate oversight by each institution or payment institution is possible, including by receiving, at least annually and upon request from the centralised monitoring function, reports that include, at least, a summary of the risk assessment and performance monitoring.

sections.title: Waiver application for consolidated entities
sections.summary: Where waivers have been granted under Article 21 of Directive 2013/36/EU or Article 109(1) of Directive 2013/36/EU in conjunction with Article 7 of Regulation (EU) No 575/2013, the parent undertaking in a Member State applies these guidelines for itself and its subsidiaries or the central body applies them for its affiliates as a whole. This applies to entities with specific waiver statuses.

requirements.text: Parent undertaking in a Member State applies guidelines for itself and subsidiaries

Where waivers have been granted pursuant to Article 21 of Directive 2013/36/EU or Article 109(1) of Directive 2013/36/EU in conjunction with Article 7 of Regulation (EU) No 575/2013, the provisions of these guidelines should be applied by the parent undertaking in a Member State for itself and its subsidiaries or by the central body and its affiliates as a whole.

sections.title: Individual compliance for subsidiaries
sections.summary: Subsidiaries of EU parent undertakings or parent undertakings in Member States without waivers must comply with these guidelines individually. This applies to entities that are not part of consolidated oversight frameworks under specific waiver conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures subsidiaries maintain individual compliance with outsourcing guidelines, affecting data transfer and processing when third parties are involved across borders.

requirements.text: Subsidiaries must comply with guidelines individually

sections.source_quote: 25. Institutions and payment institutions that are subsidiaries of an EU parent undertaking or of a parent undertaking in a Member State to which no waivers have been granted on the basis of Article 21 of Directive 2013/36/EU or Article 109(1) of Directive 2013/36/EU in conjunction with Article 7 of Regulation (EU) No 575/2013 should ensure that they comply with these Guidelines on an individual basis.

sections.title: Outsourcing function assessment: third-party arrangement classification
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must determine if their third-party arrangements constitute outsourcing. This involves evaluating whether the outsourced function is performed recurrently by the service provider and whether it would typically be handled by the institution itself, even if not previously performed.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to assess if a third-party arrangement qualifies as outsourcing before proceeding with data transfer or processing activities involving external service providers.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should establish whether an arrangement with a third party falls under the definition of outsourcing
Consider whether the function (or part thereof) outsourced to a service provider is performed on a recurrent or ongoing basis
Determine if the function (or part thereof) would normally fall within the scope of functions that could be performed by institutions or payment institutions

sections.source_quote: 26. Institutions and payment institutions should establish whether an arrangement with a third party falls under the definition of outsourcing. Within this assessment, consideration should be given to whether the function (or a part thereof) that is outsourced to a service provider is performed on a recurrent or an ongoing basis by the service provider and whether this function (or part thereof) would normally fall within the scope of functions that would or could realistically be performed by institutions or payment institutions, even if the institution or payment institution has not performed this function in the past itself.
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing assessment critical for GDPR compliance

sections.heading: Each form of outsourcing has its specific risks and advantages. Without prejudice to the waivers
sections.title: Multi-function outsourcing assessment: holistic evaluation
sections.summary: When outsourcing covers multiple functions, institutions must evaluate all aspects of the arrangement together. This includes considering combined services like data storage hardware and data backup as a single unit rather than separate components.
sections.dtia_summary: This section emphasizes that institutions should assess multi-function outsourcing arrangements as a whole to ensure comprehensive data transfer and processing safeguards.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should consider all aspects of the arrangement within their assessment
Treat combined services like data storage hardware and data backup as a single unit for assessment

sections.source_quote: 27. Where an arrangement with a service provider covers multiple functions, institutions and payment institutions should consider all aspects of the arrangement within their assessment, e.g. if the service provided includes the provision of data storage hardware and the backup of data, both aspects should be considered together.
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing assessment for cross-border data transfers

sections.heading: Where institutions and payment institutions intend to outsource important or critical functions
sections.title: Exclusions from outsourcing: non-outsourced functions
sections.summary: Institutions should not classify the following as outsourcing: statutory audit functions, market information services, global network infrastructures, clearing and settlement arrangements, global financial messaging infrastructures, correspondent banking services, and various other services and goods like cleaning, legal advice, and utilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines specific functions and services that are excluded from outsourcing, which helps institutions avoid unnecessary data transfer and processing risks when dealing with third parties.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should not consider statutory audit as outsourcing
Institutions and payment institutions should not consider market information services as outsourcing
Institutions and payment institutions should not consider global network infrastructures as outsourcing
Institutions and payment institutions should not consider clearing and settlement arrangements as outsourcing
Institutions and payment institutions should not consider global financial messaging infrastructures as outsourcing
Institutions and payment institutions should not consider correspondent banking services as outsourcing
Institutions and payment institutions should not consider services like legal advice, cleaning, and utilities as outsourcing

sections.source_quote: 28. As a general principle, institutions and payment institutions should not consider the following as outsourcing: a. a function that is legally required to be performed by a service provider, e.g. statutory audit; b. market information services (e.g. provision of data by Bloomberg, Moody's, Standard & Poor's, Fitch); c. global network infrastructures (e.g. Visa, MasterCard); d. clearing and settlement arrangements between clearing houses, central counterparties and settlement institutions and their members; e. global financial messaging infrastructures that are subject to oversight by relevant authorities; f. correspondent banking services; and g. the acquisition of services that would otherwise not be undertaken by the institution or payment institution (e.g. advice from an architect, providing legal opinion and representation in front of the court and administrative bodies, cleaning, gardening and maintenance of the institution's or payment institution's premises, medical services, servicing of company cars, catering, vending machine services, clerical services, travel services, post-room services, receptionists, secretaries and switchboard operators), goods (e.g. plastic cards, card readers, office supplies, personal computers, furniture) or utilities (e.g. electricity, gas, water, telephone line).
sections.relevance_reason: Excludes non-outsourced functions critical for data transfers

sections.heading: The same aspects that are relevant for outsourcing within a group hold true when institutions
sections.title: Critical or important functions: specific scenarios
sections.summary: Functions are considered critical or important if a defect or failure would materially impair compliance with regulatory obligations, financial performance, banking service continuity, internal control functions, or require authorisation from a competent authority. This includes specific regulatory contexts and operational impacts.
sections.dtia_summary: This section identifies when functions become critical or important for data transfer and processing, ensuring that high-risk functions are properly assessed and managed in outsourcing arrangements.

requirements.text: Functions are critical or important if a defect or failure would materially impair compliance with regulatory obligations
Functions are critical or important if a defect or failure would materially impair financial performance
Functions are critical or important if a defect or failure would materially impair banking and payment service continuity
Functions are critical or important when outsourcing internal control functions without adverse impact on effectiveness
Functions are critical or important when outsourcing banking activities or payment services requiring authorisation from a competent authority

sections.source_quote: 29. Institutions and payment institutions should always consider a function as critical or important in the following situations: a. where a defect or failure in its performance would materially impair: i. their continuing compliance with the conditions of their authorisation or its other obligations under Directive 2013/36/EU, Regulation (EU) No 575/2013, Directive 2014/65/EU, Directive (EU) 2015/2366 and Directive 2009/110/EC and their regulatory obligations; ii. their financial performance; iii. the soundness or continuity of their banking and payment services and activities; b. when operational tasks of internal control functions are outsourced, unless the assessment establishes that a failure to provide the outsourced function or the inappropriate provision of the outsourced function would not have an adverse impact on the effectiveness of the internal control function; c. when they intend to outsource functions of banking activities or payment services to an extent that would require authorisation by a competent authority, as referred to in Section 12.1.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical functions for data compliance

sections.heading: Outsourcing critical or important functions to service providers located in third countries must
sections.title: Critical functions for core business lines
sections.summary: Functions necessary for core business lines or critical functions should be considered critical or important for outsourcing assessments, unless the institution can demonstrate that failure or inappropriate provision would not impact operational continuity.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies that core business line functions are critical for data transfer and processing, ensuring that essential services are maintained during outsourcing arrangements.

requirements.text: Functions necessary to perform activities of core business lines or critical functions should be considered critical or important

sections.summary: When determining if an outsourcing arrangement involves a critical or important function, institutions must evaluate factors including direct connection to authorized banking activities, potential financial and operational impacts, risk management capabilities, client service effects, aggregated exposure to service providers, business area complexity, scalability, substitutability, and reintegration feasibility.

sections.source_quote: 31. When assessing whether an outsourcing arrangement relates to a function that is critical or important, institutions and payment institutions should take into account, together with the outcome of the risk assessment outlined in Section 12.2, at least the following factors: a. whether the outsourcing arrangement is directly connected to the provision of banking activities or payment services for which they are authorised; b. the potential impact of any disruption to the outsourced function or failure of the service provider to provide the service at the agreed service levels on a continuous basis on their: i. short- and long-term financial resilience and viability, including, if applicable, its assets, capital, costs, funding, liquidity, profits and losses; ii. business continuity and operational resilience; iii. operational risk, including conduct, information and communication technology (ICT) and legal risks; iv. reputational risks; v. where applicable, recovery and resolution planning, resolvability and operational continuity in an early intervention, recovery or resolution situation; c. the potential impact of the outsourcing arrangement on their ability to: i. identify, monitor and manage all risks; ii. comply with all legal and regulatory requirements; iii. conduct appropriate audits regarding the outsourced function; d. the potential impact on the services provided to its clients; e. all outsourcing arrangements, the institution's or payment institution's aggregated exposure to the same service provider and the potential cumulative impact of outsourcing arrangements in the same business area; f. the size and complexity of any business area affected; g. the possibility that the proposed outsourcing arrangement might be scaled up without replacing or revising the underlying agreement; h. the ability to transfer the proposed outsourcing arrangement to another service provider, if necessary or desirable, both contractually and in practice, including the estimated risks, impediments to business continuity, costs and time frame for doing so ('substitutability'); i. the ability to reintegrate the outsourced function into the institution or payment institution, if necessary or desirable; j. the protection of data and the potential impact of a confidentiality breach or failure to ensure data availability and integrity on the institution or payment institution and its clients, including but not limited to compliance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

sections.heading: When assessing whether an outsourcing arrangement relates to a function that is critical or
sections.title: Critical function assessment factors
sections.summary: When determining if an outsourcing arrangement involves a critical or important function, institutions must evaluate factors including direct connection to authorized banking activities, potential financial and operational impacts, risk management capabilities, client service effects, aggregated exposure to service providers, business area complexity, scalability, substitutability, and reintegration feasibility.

requirements.text: Assess whether outsourcing arrangement is directly connected to authorized banking activities or payment services
Evaluate potential impact of disruption on financial resilience, business continuity, operational risk, reputational risk, and recovery planning

Assess aggregated exposure to the same service provider and cumulative impact in business areas
Evaluate size and complexity of affected business areas
Assess possibility of scaling up outsourcing without revising agreements
Evaluate ability to transfer outsourcing arrangement to another service provider
Evaluate ability to reintegrate outsourced functions

sections.source_quote: 31. When assessing whether an outsourcing arrangement relates to a function that is critical or important, institutions and payment institutions should take into account, together with the outcome of the risk assessment outlined in Section 12.2, at least the following factors: a. whether the outsourcing arrangement is directly connected to the provision of banking activities or payment services for which they are authorised; b. the potential impact of any disruption to the outsourced function or failure of the service provider to provide the service at the agreed service levels on a continuous basis on their: i. short- and long-term financial resilience and viability, including, if applicable, its assets, capital, costs, funding, liquidity, profits and losses; ii. business continuity and operational resilience; iii. operational risk, including conduct, information and communication technology (ICT) and legal risks; iv. reputational risks; v. where applicable, recovery and resolution planning, resolvability and operational continuity in an early intervention, recovery or resolution situation; c. the potential impact of the outsourcing arrangement on their ability to: i. identify, monitor and manage all risks; ii. comply with all legal and regulatory requirements; iii. conduct appropriate audits regarding the outsourced function; d. the potential impact on the services provided to its clients; e. all outsourcing arrangements, the institution's or payment institution's aggregated exposure to the same service provider and the potential cumulative impact of outsourcing arrangements in the same business area; f. the size and complexity of any business area affected; g. the possibility that the proposed outsourcing arrangement might be scaled up without replacing or revising the underlying agreement; h. the ability to transfer the proposed outsourcing arrangement to another service provider, if necessary or desirable, both contractually and in practice, including the estimated risks, impediments to business continuity, costs and time frame for doing so ('substitutability'); i. the ability to reintegrate the outsourced function into the institution or payment institution, if necessary or desirable; j. the protection of data and the potential impact of a confidentiality breach or failure to ensure data availability and integrity on the institution or payment institution and its clients, including but not limited to compliance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

sections.source_quote: 38. Institutions and payment institutions should: a. clearly assign the responsibilities for the documentation, management and control of outsourcing arrangements; b. allocate sufficient resources to ensure compliance with all legal and regulatory requirements, including these guidelines and the documentation and monitoring of all outsourcing arrangements; c. taking into account Section 1 of these guidelines, establish an outsourcing function or designate a senior staff member who is directly accountable to the management body (e.g. a key function holder of a control function) and responsible for managing and overseeing the risks of outsourcing arrangements as part of the institutions internal control framework and overseeing the documentation of outsourcing arrangements. sections.title: Maintain sufficient substance to avoid being empty shells

sections.source_quote: 39. Institutions and payment institutions should maintain at all times sufficient substance and not become ‘empty shells' or ‘letter-box entities'. To this end, they should: a. meet all the conditions of their authorisation43 at all times, including the management body effectively carrying out its responsibilities as set out in paragraph 36 of these guidelines; b. retain a clear and transparent organisational framework and structure that enables them to ensure compliance with legal and regulatory requirements; c. where operational tasks of internal control functions are outsourced (e.g. in the case of intragroup outsourcing or outsourcing within institutional protection schemes), exercise appropriate oversight and be able to manage the risks that are generated by the outsourcing of critical or important functions; and d. have sufficient resources and capacities to ensure compliance with points (a) to (c).

sections.summary: Institutions must ensure they can make decisions related to business activities and critical functions, maintain business orderliness, identify and manage outsourcing risks, implement confidentiality arrangements, maintain information flow with service providers, and have exit strategies for critical functions. sections.source_quote: 40. When outsourcing, institutions and payment institutions should at least ensure that: a. they can take and implement decisions related to their business activities and critical or important functions, including with regard to those that have been outsourced; b. they maintain the orderliness of the conduct of their business and the banking and payment services they provide; c. the risks related to current and planned outsourcing arrangements are adequately identified, assessed, managed and mitigated, including risks related to ICT and financial technology (fintech); d. appropriate confidentiality arrangements are in place regarding data and other information; e. an appropriate flow of relevant information with service providers is maintained; f. with regard to the outsourcing of critical or important functions, they are able to undertake at least one of the following actions, within an appropriate time frame: i. transfer the function to alternative service providers; ii. reintegrate the function; or iii. discontinue the business activities that are depending on the function. g. where personal data are processed by service providers located in the EU and/or third countries, appropriate measures are implemented and data are processed in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

sections.title: Approve and update outsourcing policy

sections.source_quote: 41. The management body of an institution or payment institution 44 that has outsourcing arrangements in place or plans on entering into such arrangements should approve, regularly review and update a written outsourcing policy and ensure its implementation, as applicable, on an individual, sub-consolidated and consolidated basis. For institutions, the outsourcing policy should be in accordance with Section 8 of the EBA's Guidelines on internal governance and, in particular, should take into account the requirements set out in Section 18 (new products and significant changes) of those guidelines. Payment institutions may also align their policies with Sections 8 and 18 of the EBA Guidelines on internal governance.

sections.title: Define outsourcing policy lifecycle phases

It must address critical functions, risk management, due diligence, and business continuity.

sections.source_quote: 42. The policy should include the main phases of the life cycle of outsourcing arrangements and define the principles, responsibilities and processes in relation to outsourcing. In particular, the policy should cover at least: a. the responsibilities of the management body in line with paragraph 36, including its involvement, as appropriate, in the decision-making on outsourcing of critical or important functions; b. the involvement of business lines, internal control functions and other individuals in respect of outsourcing arrangements; c. the planning of outsourcing arrangements, including: i. the definition of business requirements regarding outsourcing arrangements; ii. the criteria, including those referred to in Section 4, and processes for identifying critical or important functions; iii. risk identification, assessment and management in accordance with Section 12.2; iv. due diligence checks on prospective service providers, including the measures required under Section 12.3; v. procedures for the identification, assessment, management and mitigation of potential conflicts of interest, in accordance with Section 8; vi. business continuity planning in accordance with Section 9; vii. the approval process of new outsourcing arrangements; d. the implementation, monitoring and management of outsourcing arrangements, including: i. the ongoing assessment of the service provider's performance in line with Section 14; ii. the procedures for being notified and responding to changes to an outsourcing arrangement or service provider (e.g. to its financial position, organisational or ownership structures, sub-outsourcing); iii. the independent review and audit of compliance with legal and regulatory requirements and policies; iv. the renewal processes; e. the documentation and record-keeping, taking into account the requirements in Section 11; f. the exit strategies and termination processes, including a requirement for a documented exit plan for each critical or important function to be outsourced where such an exit is considered possible taking into account possible service interruptions or the unexpected termination of an outsourcing agreement.

sections.source_quote: 43. The register should include at least the following information for all existing outsourcing arrangements: a. a reference number for each outsourcing arrangement; b. the start date and, as applicable, the next contract renewal date, the end date and/or notice periods for the service provider and for the institution or payment institution; c. a brief description of the outsourced function, including the data that are outsourced and whether or not personal data (e.g. by providing a yes or no in a separate data field) have been transferred or if their processing is outsourced to a service provider; d. a category assigned by the institution or payment institution that reflects the nature of the function as described under point (c) (e.g. information technology (IT), control function), which should facilitate the identification of different types of arrangements; e. the name of the service provider, the corporate registration number, the legal entity identifier (where available), the registered address and other relevant contact details, and the name of its parent company (if any); f. the country or countries where the service is to be performed, including the location (i.e. country or region) of the data; g. whether or not (yes/no) the outsourced function is considered critical or important, including, where applicable, a brief summary of the reasons why the outsourced function is considered critical or important; h. in the case of outsourcing to a cloud service provider, the cloud service and deployment models, i.e. public/private/hybrid/community, and the specific nature of the data to be held and the locations (i.e. country or regions) where such data will be stored; i. the date of the most recent assessment of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function.

requirements.text: Document assessments made under Title IV
Document results of ongoing monitoring

sections.source_quote: 60. Institutions and payment institutions should appropriately document the assessments made under Title IV and the results of their ongoing monitoring (e.g. performance of the service provider, compliance with agreed service levels, other contractual and regulatory requirements, updates to the risk assessment).
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing assessment documentation critical for compliance

sections.heading: Before entering into any outsourcing arrangement, institutions and payment institutions
sections.title: Pre-outsourcing assessment requirements
sections.summary: Before entering any outsourcing arrangement, institutions must assess whether it concerns critical or important functions, meet supervisory conditions, identify relevant risks, conduct due diligence on service providers, and assess conflicts of interest. These steps ensure compliance with regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive pre-outsourcing assessments, impacting data transfer assessments by mandating thorough risk evaluations before third-party data processing arrangements are established.

requirements.text: Assess if outsourcing arrangement concerns critical or important function
Assess if supervisory conditions for outsourcing are met
Identify and assess all relevant risks of outsourcing arrangement
Undertake appropriate due diligence on prospective service provider
Identify and assess conflicts of interest

sections.source_quote: 61. Before entering into any outsourcing arrangement, institutions and payment institutions should: a. assess if the outsourcing arrangement concerns a critical or important function, as set out in Title II; b. assess if the supervisory conditions for outsourcing set out in Section 12.1 are met; c. identify and assess all of the relevant risks of the outsourcing arrangement in accordance with Section 12.2; d. undertake appropriate due diligence on the prospective service provider in accordance with Section 12.3; e. identify and assess conflicts of interest that the outsourcing may cause in line with Section 8.
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing and data protection requirements

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the outsourcing of functions of
sections.title: Outsourcing banking functions to service providers in same or other Member State
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure that outsourcing banking activities or payment services requiring authorization or registration in their home Member State to service providers in the same or another Member State only occurs if the service provider is authorized or registered to perform those activities in that Member State, or is otherwise permitted to carry out those activities under national law.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing to service providers in other Member States requires authorization or registration in the host Member State, or compliance with national legal frameworks for the same activities. This affects cross-border data transfers by requiring local regulatory alignment.

requirements.text: Service provider must be authorized or registered by a competent authority to perform banking activities or payment services
Service provider must be otherwise allowed to carry out banking activities or payment services in accordance with relevant national legal framework

sections.source_quote: 62. Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the outsourcing of functions of banking activities or payment services, to an extent that the performance of that function requires authorisation or registration by a competent authority in the Member State where they are authorised, to a service provider located in the same or another Member State takes place only if one of the following conditions is met: a. the service provider is authorised or registered by a competent authority to perform such banking activities or payment services; or b. the service provider is otherwise allowed to carry out those banking activities or payment services in accordance with the relevant national legal framework.
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing and cross-border financial data compliance

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the outsourcing of functions of
sections.title: Outsourcing banking functions to service providers in third countries
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure that outsourcing banking activities or payment services requiring authorization or registration in their home Member State to service providers in third countries only occurs if the service provider is authorized or registered in that third country, there is a cooperation agreement between supervisory authorities, and the agreement ensures access to information, data, premises, personnel, and timely breach notifications for regulatory oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing to third countries requires a supervisory cooperation agreement to ensure regulatory oversight, which impacts data transfers by mandating cross-border regulatory coordination.

requirements.text: Service provider must be authorized or registered to provide banking activity or payment service in the third country and supervised by relevant competent authority
There must be an appropriate cooperation agreement between supervisory authorities
Cooperation agreement must ensure access to information necessary for supervisory tasks
Cooperation agreement must ensure access to relevant data, documents, premises or personnel
Cooperation agreement must ensure timely receipt of information on potential breaches
Cooperation agreement must ensure cooperation on enforcement in case of regulatory breaches

sections.source_quote: 63. Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the outsourcing of functions of banking activities or payment services, to an extent that the performance of that function requires authorisation or registration by a competent authority in the Member State where they are authorised, to a service provider located in a third country takes place only if the following conditions are met: a. the service provider is authorised or registered to provide that banking activity or payment service in the third country and is supervised by a relevant competent authority in that third country (referred to as a ‘supervisory authority'); b. there is an appropriate cooperation agreement, e.g. in the form of a memorandum of understanding or college agreement, between the competent authorities responsible for the supervision of the institution and the supervisory authorities responsible for the supervision of the service provider; and c. the cooperation agreement referred to in point (b) should ensure that the competent authorities are able, at least, to: i. obtain, upon request, the information necessary to carry out their supervisory tasks pursuant to Directive 2013/36/EU, Regulation (EU) No 575/2013, Directive (EU) 2015/2366 and Directive 2009/110/EC; ii. obtain appropriate access to any data, documents, premises or personnel in the third country that are relevant for the performance of their supervisory powers; iii. receive, as soon as possible, information from the supervisory authority in the third country for investigating apparent breaches of the requirements of Directive 2013/36/EU, Regulation (EU) No 575/2013, Directive (EU) 2015/2366 and Directive 2009/110/EC; and iv. cooperate with the relevant supervisory authorities in the third country on enforcement in the case of a breach of the applicable regulatory requirements and national law in the Member State.
sections.relevance_reason: Core cross-border outsourcing and financial data compliance

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should assess the potential impact of outsourcing
sections.title: Assessing operational risk impact of outsourcing arrangements
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must assess how outsourcing arrangements affect operational risk, consider these results when deciding whether to outsource functions, and take steps to avoid undue operational risks before entering into outsourcing agreements.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing arrangements must be evaluated for operational risk impact to prevent unintended consequences on financial stability and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions must assess potential impact of outsourcing arrangements on operational risk
Institutions and payment institutions must consider assessment results when deciding if function should be outsourced
Institutions and payment institutions must take appropriate steps to avoid undue additional operational risks before entering into outsourcing arrangements

sections.source_quote: 64. Institutions and payment institutions should assess the potential impact of outsourcing arrangements on their operational risk, should take into account the assessment results when deciding if the function should be outsourced to a service provider and should take appropriate steps to avoid undue additional operational risks before entering into outsourcing arrangements.
sections.relevance_reason: Operational risk impact on financial data outsourcing

sections.heading: The assessment should include, where appropriate, scenarios of possible risk events, including
sections.title: Scenario analysis for risk events in outsourcing
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must include scenarios of high-severity operational risk events in their outsourcing risk assessments, evaluate potential impacts of failed or inadequate services, and document analysis results while considering institutional size and complexity to determine how outsourcing affects operational risk.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing risk assessments must include scenario analysis for operational failures to ensure robustness of critical functions across borders.

requirements.text: Assessment should include scenarios of possible risk events, including high-severity operational risk events
Institutions and payment institutions should assess potential impact of failed or inadequate services
Institutions and payment institutions should document analysis performed and results
Institutions and payment institutions should estimate extent to which outsourcing arrangement increases or decreases operational risk
Small and non-complex institutions may use qualitative risk assessment approaches
Large or complex institutions should have sophisticated risk assessment approaches

sections.source_quote: 65. The assessment should include, where appropriate, scenarios of possible risk events, including high-severity operational risk events. Within the scenario analysis, institutions and payment institutions should assess the potential impact of failed or inadequate services, including the risks caused by processes, systems, people or external events. Institutions and payment institutions, taking into account the principle of proportionality referred to in Section 1, should document the analysis performed and their results and should estimate the extent to which the outsourcing arrangement would increase or decrease their operational risk. Taking into account Title I, small and non-complex institutions and payment institutions may use qualitative risk assessment approaches, while large or complex institutions should have a more sophisticated approach, including, where available, the use of internal and external loss data to inform the scenario analysis.
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing risk assessment for financial data under GDPR

sections.heading: Within the risk assessment, institutions and payments institutions should also take into account
sections.title: Risk assessment of outsourcing benefits and costs
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must evaluate the expected benefits and costs of outsourcing arrangements, including weighing risks reduced or better managed against new risks introduced by outsourcing, while considering concentration risks, aggregated risks, step-in risks, and risk mitigation measures.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing risk assessments must balance benefits and costs while addressing concentration, aggregation, and step-in risks to ensure financial stability.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions must assess expected benefits and costs of proposed outsourcing arrangements
Institutions and payment institutions must weigh risks reduced or better managed against risks arising from outsourcing
Institutions and payment institutions must consider concentration risks from dominant service providers
Institutions and payment institutions must consider aggregated risks from multiple outsourced functions
Institutions and payment institutions must consider step-in risks for significant institutions
Institutions and payment institutions must consider risk mitigation measures implemented by themselves and service providers

sections.source_quote: 66. Within the risk assessment, institutions and payments institutions should also take into account the expected benefits and costs of the proposed outsourcing arrangement, including weighing any risks that may be reduced or better managed against any risks that may arise as a result of the proposed outsourcing arrangement, taking into account at least: a. concentration risks, including from: i. outsourcing to a dominant service provider that is not easily substitutable; and ii. multiple outsourcing arrangements with the same service provider or closely connected service providers; b. the aggregated risks resulting from outsourcing several functions across the institution or payment institution and, in the case of groups of institutions or institutional protection schemes, the aggregated risks on a consolidated basis or on the basis of the institutional protection scheme; c. in the case of significant institutions, the step-in risk, i.e. the risk that may result from the need to provide financial support to a service provider in distress or to take over its business operations; and d. the measures implemented by the institution or payment institution and by the service provider to manage and mitigate the risks.
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing risk assessment for financial data under GDPR

sections.heading: Where the outsourcing arrangement includes the possibility that the service provider sub-
sections.title: Sub-outsourcing critical functions and risks
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must consider risks associated with sub-outsourcing critical or important functions, including additional risks from sub-contractors in third countries or different countries from the service provider, and the reduced ability to oversee functions and supervise them effectively.
sections.dtia_summary: Sub-outsourcing critical functions introduces additional risks that must be evaluated to ensure effective oversight and regulatory compliance across borders.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions must take into account risks associated with sub-outsourcing critical or important functions
Institutions and payment institutions must consider additional risks if sub-contractor is located in third country or different country from service provider
Institutions and payment institutions must consider risk that long and complex sub-outsourcing chains reduce ability to oversee functions
Institutions and payment institutions must consider risk that long and complex sub-outsourcing chains reduce ability of competent authorities to supervise

sections.source_quote: 67. Where the outsourcing arrangement includes the possibility that the service provider sub-outsources critical or important functions to other service providers, institutions and payment institutions should take into account: a. the risks associated with sub-outsourcing, including the additional risks that may arise if the sub-contractor is located in a third country or a different country from the service provider; b. the risk that long and complex chains of sub-outsourcing reduce the ability of institutions or payment institutions to oversee the outsourced critical or important function and the ability of competent authorities to effectively supervise them.
sections.relevance_reason: Sub-outsourcing critical functions risks

sections.heading: When carrying out the risk assessment prior to outsourcing and during ongoing monitoring of
sections.title: Risk assessment for outsourcing: data classification and risk analysis
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must identify and classify functions and related data systems by sensitivity and security needs before outsourcing. They must conduct a thorough risk-based analysis covering operational risks including legal, ICT, compliance, and reputational risks, as well as oversight limitations in jurisdictions where services are provided or data is stored. They must also assess the service provider's location within or outside the EU, political stability, and data protection laws in relevant jurisdictions. Additionally, they must define appropriate data confidentiality, continuity, integrity, and traceability protections, including encryption and key management for data in transit, memory, and at rest.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires detailed risk assessment of third-party service providers, including jurisdictional risks and data protection measures, which directly impacts cross-border data transfers and outsourcing arrangements.

requirements.text: Identify and classify relevant functions and related data and systems by sensitivity and required security measures
Conduct thorough risk-based analysis of functions and related data systems, addressing operational risks including legal, ICT, compliance and reputational risks
Assess consequences of service provider location within or outside the EU
Consider political stability and security of jurisdictions, including data protection laws, law enforcement provisions, and insolvency laws
Define and decide on appropriate level of protection for data confidentiality, continuity of activities, integrity and traceability of data and systems
Consider specific measures for data in transit, memory and at rest, such as encryption technologies with appropriate key management

sections.source_quote: 68. When carrying out the risk assessment prior to outsourcing and during ongoing monitoring of the service provider's performance, institutions and payment institutions should, at least: a. identify and classify the relevant functions and related data and systems as regards their sensitivity and required security measures; b. conduct a thorough risk-based analysis of the functions and related data and systems that are being considered for outsourcing or have been outsourced and address the potential risks, in particular the operational risks, including legal, ICT, compliance and reputational risks, and the oversight limitations related to the countries where the outsourced services are or may be provided and where the data are or are likely to be stored; c. consider the consequences of where the service provider is located (within or outside the EU); d. consider the political stability and security situation of the jurisdictions in question, including: i. the laws in force, including laws on data protection; ii. the law enforcement provisions in place; and iii. the insolvency law provisions that would apply in the event of a service provider's failure and any constraints that would arise in respect of the urgent recovery of the institution's or payment institution's data in particular; e. define and decide on an appropriate level of protection of data confidentiality, of continuity of the activities outsourced and of the integrity and traceability of data and systems in the context of the intended outsourcing. Institutions and payment institutions should also consider specific measures, where necessary, for data in transit, data in memory and data at rest, such as the use of encryption technologies in combination with an appropriate key management architecture;
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing risk assessment for GDPR compliance

sections.heading: Before entering into an outsourcing arrangement and considering the operational risks related
sections.title: Service provider suitability for outsourcing arrangements
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure that the service provider is suitable before entering into an outsourcing arrangement, considering operational risks related to the function being outsourced. This includes evaluating the provider's capability to meet obligations over the duration of the contract.
sections.dtia_summary: This section emphasizes the need to assess service provider suitability before outsourcing, which affects cross-border data transfers and contractual safeguards.

requirements.text: Ensure service provider is suitable before entering into outsourcing arrangement

sections.source_quote: 69. Before entering into an outsourcing arrangement and considering the operational risks related to the function to be outsourced, institutions and payment institutions should ensure in their selection and assessment process that the service provider is suitable.
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing risk assessment for financial data

sections.heading: With regard to critical and important functions, institutions and payment institutions should
sections.title: Critical functions outsourcing requirements
sections.summary: For critical and important functions, institutions and payment institutions must ensure the service provider has business reputation, sufficient abilities, expertise, capacity, resources, organizational structure, and regulatory authorizations to perform functions reliably and professionally over the contract duration.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies requirements for critical functions outsourcing, impacting data security and operational continuity in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Ensure service provider has business reputation, appropriate and sufficient abilities, expertise, capacity, resources (e.g. human, IT, financial), organizational structure, and required regulatory authorizations

sections.source_quote: 70. With regard to critical and important functions, institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the service provider has the business reputation, appropriate and sufficient abilities, the expertise, the capacity, the resources (e.g. human, IT, financial), the organisational structure and, if applicable, the required regulatory authorisation(s) or registration(s) to perform the critical or important function in a reliable and professional manner to meet its obligations over the duration of the draft contract.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical functions outsourcing for financial data under GDPR

sections.heading: Additional factors to be considered when conducting due diligence on a potential service
sections.title: Due diligence factors for service providers
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must consider additional factors during due diligence on potential service providers, including business model, financial situation, ownership structure, relationships with existing providers, and regulatory supervision status.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines due diligence factors for service providers, which are critical for assessing cross-border data transfer risks and contractual compliance.

requirements.text: Consider business model, nature, scale, complexity, financial situation, ownership and group structure of service provider
Evaluate long-term relationships with service providers that have already been assessed
Assess whether service provider is parent/subsidiary of institution, part of accounting consolidation, or member of institutional protection scheme
Determine if service provider is supervised by competent authorities

sections.source_quote: 71. Additional factors to be considered when conducting due diligence on a potential service provider include, but are not limited to: a. its business model, nature, scale, complexity, financial situation, ownership and group structure; b. the long-term relationships with service providers that have already been assessed and perform services for the institution or payment institution; c. whether the service provider is a parent undertaking or subsidiary of the institution or payment institution, is part of the accounting scope of consolidation of the institution or is a member of or is owned by institutions that are members of the same institutional protection scheme to which the institution belongs; d. whether or not the service provider is supervised by competent authorities.
sections.relevance_reason: Third-party due diligence for financial data under GDPR

sections.heading: Where outsourcing involves the processing of personal or confidential data, institutions and
sections.title: Data protection for outsourced personal data
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure that service providers implementing outsourcing of personal or confidential data have appropriate technical and organizational measures to protect the data.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates technical and organizational safeguards for outsourced personal data, directly affecting cross-border data transfer security.

requirements.text: Ensure service provider implements appropriate technical and organizational measures to protect personal or confidential data

sections.source_quote: 72. Where outsourcing involves the processing of personal or confidential data, institutions and payment institutions should be satisfied that the service provider implements appropriate technical and organisational measures to protect the data.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data protection for outsourced personal data

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should take appropriate steps to ensure that service
sections.title: Ethical and social responsibility for service providers
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure service providers act ethically and responsibly, including adherence to international human rights standards, environmental protection, and working conditions, with a prohibition on child labor.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires service providers to meet ethical standards, which may impact cross-border data transfers through compliance with international human rights frameworks.

requirements.text: Ensure service providers act in manner consistent with values and code of conduct, including ethical and socially responsible behavior
Ensure service providers adhere to international standards on human rights (e.g. European Convention on Human Rights)
Ensure service providers comply with environmental protection and appropriate working conditions, including prohibition of child labor

sections.source_quote: 73. Institutions and payment institutions should take appropriate steps to ensure that service providers act in a manner consistent with their values and code of conduct. In particular, with regard to service providers located in third countries and, if applicable, their sub-contractors, institutions and payment institutions should be satisfied that the service provider acts in an ethical and socially responsible manner and adheres to international standards on human rights (e.g. the European Convention on Human Rights), environmental protection and appropriate working conditions, including the prohibition of child labour.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: ethical/social responsibility not core data compliance

sections.heading: The outsourcing agreement for critical or important functions should set out at least:
sections.title: Outsourcing agreement requirements for critical functions
sections.summary: The outsourcing agreement for critical or important functions must specify the outsourced function, agreement duration with notice periods, governing law, financial obligations, sub-outsourcing permissions with conditions, data locations, data security measures, performance monitoring rights, service levels, reporting obligations, insurance requirements, business contingency plans, data access during insolvency, cooperation with authorities, and resolution authority powers. These obligations apply to institutions and payment institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed contractual terms for critical functions to ensure data security, continuity, and regulatory compliance during outsourcing, directly impacting cross-border data transfers and third-party processing risks.

requirements.text: A clear description of the outsourced function to be provided
Start date and end date, where applicable, of the agreement and notice periods for the service provider and institution
Governing law of the agreement
Parties' financial obligations
Whether sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions is permitted and conditions specified in Section 13.1
Location(s) where critical functions will be provided and data processed, including storage locations and conditions for location changes
Provisions regarding accessibility, availability, integrity, privacy and safety of relevant data as specified in Section 13.2
Right of institution to monitor service provider's performance on an ongoing basis
Agreed service levels with precise quantitative and qualitative performance targets
Reporting obligations of service provider to institution, including developments affecting service delivery and compliance
Whether service provider should take mandatory insurance against certain risks and requested coverage levels
Requirements to implement and test business contingency plans
Obligation to ensure data access during service provider insolvency, resolution or discontinuation
Obligation to cooperate with competent authorities and resolution authorities
Clear reference to national resolution authority's powers, especially Articles 68 and 71 of Directive 2014/59/EU
Unrestricted right of institutions to inspect and audit service provider
Termination rights as specified in Section 13.4

sections.source_quote: 75. The outsourcing agreement for critical or important functions should set out at least: a. a clear description of the outsourced function to be provided; b. the start date and end date, where applicable, of the agreement and the notice periods for the service provider and the institution or payment institution; c. the governing law of the agreement; d. the parties' financial obligations; e. whether the sub-outsourcing of a critical or important function, or material parts thereof, is permitted and, if so, the conditions specified in Section 13.1 that the sub-outsourcing is subject to; f. the location(s) (i.e. regions or countries) where the critical or important function will be provided and/or where relevant data will be kept and processed, including the possible storage location, and the conditions to be met, including a requirement to notify the institution or payment institution if the service provider proposes to change the location(s); g. where relevant, provisions regarding the accessibility, availability, integrity, privacy and safety of relevant data, as specified in Section 13.2; h. the right of the institution or payment institution to monitor the service provider's performance on an ongoing basis; i. the agreed service levels, which should include precise quantitative and qualitative performance targets for the outsourced function to allow for timely monitoring so that appropriate corrective action can be taken without undue delay if the agreed service levels are not met; j. the reporting obligations of the service provider to the institution or payment institution, including the communication by the service provider of any development that may have a material impact on the service provider's ability to effectively carry out the critical or important function in line with the agreed service levels and in compliance with applicable laws and regulatory requirements and, as appropriate, the obligations to submit reports of the internal audit function of the service provider; k. whether the service provider should take mandatory insurance against certain risks and, if applicable, the level of insurance cover requested;
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing critical functions with data security and breach requirements

sections.heading: The outsourcing agreement should specify whether or not sub-outsourcing of critical or
sections.title: Sub-outsourcing permission for critical functions
sections.summary: The outsourcing agreement must specify whether sub-outsourcing critical or important functions, or material parts thereof, is permitted. This applies to institutions and payment institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires explicit permission for sub-outsourcing critical functions, which affects data transfer risks and third-party processing compliance.

requirements.text: Specify whether sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions, or material parts thereof, is permitted

sections.source_quote: 76. The outsourcing agreement should specify whether or not sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions, or material parts thereof, is permitted.
sections.relevance_reason: Sub-outsourcing critical functions requires explicit permission for data security

sections.heading: If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions is permitted, institutions and payment
sections.title: Sub-outsourcing critical function materiality assessment
sections.summary: If sub-outsourcing critical or important functions is permitted, institutions and payment institutions must determine whether the sub-outsourceable part is critical or important and record it in their register.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires institutions to assess and document the criticality of sub-outsourceable functions, impacting data transfer risk management.

requirements.text: Determine whether the part of the function to be sub-outsourced is critical or important (i.e. a material part of the critical or important function)
