Record the determination in the register

sections.source_quote: 77. If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions is permitted, institutions and payment institutions should determine whether the part of the function to be sub-outsourced is, as such, critical or important (i.e. a material part of the critical or important function) and, if so, record it in the register.
sections.relevance_reason: Sub-outsourcing critical function materiality assessment directly impacts data protection and cross-border data transfer

sections.heading: If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions is permitted, the written agreement should:
sections.title: Sub-outsourcing agreement conditions
sections.summary: If sub-outsourcing critical or important functions is permitted, the agreement must specify excluded activities, sub-outsourcing conditions, service provider oversight obligations, prior authorization requirements for data sub-outsourcing, notification of planned sub-outsourcing, and contractual rights to object or terminate.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines contractual safeguards for sub-outsourcing, critical for managing data transfer risks and ensuring compliance with financial regulations.

requirements.text: Specify any types of activities excluded from sub-outsourcing
Specify conditions to be complied with in the case of sub-outsourcing
Require service provider to oversee subcontracted services to ensure contractual obligations are met
Require service provider to obtain prior specific or general written authorization from institution before sub-outsourcing data
Oblige service provider to inform institution of planned sub-outsourcing or material changes
Ensure institution has right to object to intended sub-outsourcing or material changes
Ensure institution has contractual right to terminate agreement in case of undue sub-outsourcing

sections.source_quote: 78. If sub-outsourcing of critical or important functions is permitted, the written agreement should: a. specify any types of activities that are excluded from sub-outsourcing; b. specify the conditions to be complied with in the case of sub-outsourcing; c. specify that the service provider is obliged to oversee those services that it has subcontracted to ensure that all contractual obligations between the service provider and the institution or payment institution are continuously met; d. require the service provider to obtain prior specific or general written authorisation from the institution or payment institution before sub-outsourcing data; e. include an obligation of the service provider to inform the institution or payment institution of any planned sub-outsourcing, or material changes thereof, in particular where that might affect the ability of the service provider to meet its responsibilities under the outsourcing agreement. This includes planned significant changes of subcontractors and to the notification period; in particular, the notification period to be set should allow the outsourcing institution or payment institution at least to carry out a risk assessment of the proposed changes and to object to changes before the planned sub-outsourcing, or material changes thereof, come into effect; f. ensure, where appropriate, that the institution or payment institution has the right to object to intended sub-outsourcing, or material changes thereof, or that explicit approval is required; g. ensure that the institution or payment institution has the contractual right to terminate the agreement in the case of undue sub-outsourcing, e.g. where the sub-outsourcing materially increases the risks for the institution or payment institution or where the service provider sub-outsources without notifying the institution or payment institution.
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing security requirements for GDPR

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should agree to sub-outsourcing only if the sub-
sections.title: Sub-contractor compliance obligations
sections.summary: Subcontractors must comply with all applicable laws, regulatory requirements, and contractual obligations, and grant institutions and payment institutions the same contractual rights of access and audit as those granted by the service provider.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures subcontractors maintain equivalent compliance and access rights, critical for data transfer integrity and regulatory oversight.

requirements.text: Comply with all applicable laws, regulatory requirements and contractual obligations
Grant institution, payment institution and competent authority the same contractual rights of access and audit as those granted by the service provider

sections.source_quote: 79. Institutions and payment institutions should agree to sub-outsourcing only if the subcontractor undertakes to: a. comply with all applicable laws, regulatory requirements and contractual obligations; and b. grant the institution, payment institution and competent authority the same contractual rights of access and audit as those granted by the service provider.
sections.relevance_reason: Subcontractor compliance obligations directly impact data transfers and security

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the service provider appropriately
sections.title: Sub-outsourcing risk assessment and termination rights
sections.summary: Institutions must ensure service providers appropriately oversee sub-service providers, and may object or terminate contracts if sub-outsourcing could materially affect the outsourcing arrangement or increase risks, especially where sub-contractor compliance conditions are not met.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates risk assessment for sub-outsourcing, directly impacting data transfer safety and regulatory compliance.

requirements.text: Ensure service provider appropriately oversees sub-service providers in line with institution policy
Exercise right to object or terminate contract if sub-outsourcing could have material adverse effects or lead to material risk increase

sections.source_quote: 80. Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the service provider appropriately oversees the sub-service providers, in line with the policy defined by the institution or payment institution. If the sub-outsourcing proposed could have material adverse effects on the outsourcing arrangement of a critical or important function or would lead to a material increase of risk, including where the conditions in paragraph 79 would not be met, the institution or payment institution should exercise its right to object to the sub-outsourcing, if such a right was agreed, and/or terminate the contract.
sections.relevance_reason: Sub-outsourcing risk assessment directly impacts data security and breach risks

sections.heading: Where relevant (e.g. in the context of cloud or other ICT outsourcing), institutions and payment
sections.title: Outsourcing security requirements for data and systems
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must define data and system security requirements within outsourcing agreements and continuously monitor compliance with these requirements. This applies to cloud or other ICT outsourcing arrangements involving personal data. The obligations ensure ongoing security oversight of outsourced functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit security requirements in outsourcing agreements and ongoing compliance monitoring for data and systems, critical for assessing security risks in cross-border data transfers via cloud services.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should define data and system security requirements within the outsourcing agreement
Institutions and payment institutions should monitor compliance with these security requirements on an ongoing basis

sections.source_quote: 82. Where relevant (e.g. in the context of cloud or other ICT outsourcing), institutions and payment institutions should define data and system security requirements within the outsourcing agreement and monitor compliance with these requirements on an ongoing basis.
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing security requirements for data

sections.heading: In the case of outsourcing to cloud service providers and other outsourcing arrangements that
sections.title: Risk-based approach to data storage and processing locations
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must adopt a risk-based approach to determine data storage and processing locations (countries or regions) and information security considerations when outsourcing to cloud service providers or other arrangements handling personal data. This ensures appropriate risk management for data transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates a risk-based assessment of data storage and processing locations for outsourced data, crucial for DTIA to evaluate jurisdictional risks in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should adopt a risk-based approach to data storage and data processing location(s) (i.e. country or region)
Institutions and payment institutions should adopt a risk-based approach to information security considerations

sections.source_quote: 83. In the case of outsourcing to cloud service providers and other outsourcing arrangements that involve the handling or transfer of personal or confidential data, institutions and payment institutions should adopt a risk-based approach to data storage and data processing location(s) (i.e. country or region) and information security considerations.
sections.relevance_reason: Risk-based approach to data storage/processing locations for outsourcing

sections.heading: Without prejudice to the requirements under the Regulation (EU) 2016/679, institutions and
sections.title: Data protection obligations in outsourcing agreements
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure outsourcing agreements include obligations for service providers to protect confidential, personal, or sensitive information and comply with all legal data protection requirements applicable to the institution. This includes banking secrecy and similar confidentiality duties where applicable.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires service providers to adhere to the institution's data protection standards, including confidentiality obligations, critical for DTIA to assess compliance with data protection laws in cross-border outsourcing.

requirements.text: Outsourcing agreements must include the obligation that the service provider protects confidential, personal or sensitive information
Service providers must comply with all legal requirements regarding the protection of data applicable to the institution
Service providers must observe banking secrecy or similar legal confidentiality duties with respect to clients' information where applicable

sections.source_quote: 84. Without prejudice to the requirements under the Regulation (EU) 2016/679, institutions and payment institutions, when outsourcing (in particular to third countries), should take into account differences in national provisions regarding the protection of data. Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the outsourcing agreement includes the obligation that the service provider protects confidential, personal or otherwise sensitive information and complies with all legal requirements regarding the protection of data that apply to the institution or payment institution (e.g. the protection of personal data and that banking secrecy or similar legal confidentiality duties with respect to clients' information, where applicable, are observed).
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing data protection obligations

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure within the written outsourcing
sections.title: Internal audit access for outsourced functions
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure written outsourcing agreements grant their internal audit function the ability to review outsourced functions using a risk-based approach. This enables continuous oversight of outsourced activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires internal audit access to outsourced functions for risk-based monitoring, essential for DTIA to verify compliance with security and data protection obligations in outsourcing arrangements.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure within the written outsourcing arrangement that the internal audit function can review the outsourced function using a risk-based approach

sections.source_quote: 85. Institutions and payment institutions should ensure within the written outsourcing arrangement that the internal audit function is able to review the outsourced function using a risk-based approach.
sections.relevance_reason: Internal audit access for outsourced functions ensures data security oversight

sections.heading: Regardless of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function, the written outsourcing
sections.title: Regulatory access rights for outsourced functions
sections.summary: Outsourcing agreements must grant institutions and payment institutions, along with their competent authorities and resolution authorities, the right to access information and conduct audits of service providers in both Member States and third countries. This ensures regulatory oversight of outsourced functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates regulatory access rights for monitoring outsourced functions across jurisdictions, critical for DTIA to assess compliance with data protection laws in cross-border outsourcing.

requirements.text: Outsourcing agreements must refer to information gathering and investigatory powers of competent authorities and resolution authorities

sections.source_quote: 86. Regardless of the criticality or importance of the outsourced function, the written outsourcing arrangements between institutions and service providers should refer to the information gathering and investigatory powers of competent authorities and resolution authorities under Article 63(1)(a) of Directive 2014/59/EU and Article 65(3) of Directive 2013/36/EU with regard to service providers located in a Member State and should also ensure those rights with regard to service providers located in third countries.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory access for outsourced data functions

sections.heading: With regard to the outsourcing of critical or important functions, institutions and payment
sections.title: Access and audit rights for outsourced functions
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure outsourcing agreements grant full access to business premises, systems, data, and personnel for audits, as well as unrestricted inspection and auditing rights to monitor compliance with regulatory and contractual requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires comprehensive access and audit rights for regulatory oversight of outsourced functions, vital for DTIA to verify data protection compliance in cross-border outsourcing arrangements.

requirements.text: Service providers must grant full access to all relevant business premises, including devices, systems, networks, information, and data used for providing the outsourced function
Service providers must grant unrestricted rights of inspection and auditing related to the outsourcing arrangement

sections.source_quote: 87. With regard to the outsourcing of critical or important functions, institutions and payment institutions should ensure within the written outsourcing agreement that the service provider grants them and their competent authorities, including resolution authorities, and any other person appointed by them or the competent authorities, the following: a. full access to all relevant business premises (e.g. head offices and operation centres), including the full range of relevant devices, systems, networks, information and data used for providing the outsourced function, including related financial information, personnel and the service provider's external auditors (‘access and information rights'); and b. unrestricted rights of inspection and auditing related to the outsourcing arrangement (‘audit rights'), to enable them to monitor the outsourcing arrangement and to ensure compliance with all applicable regulatory and contractual requirements.
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing audit rights for data access

sections.heading: For the outsourcing of functions that are not critical or important, institutions and payment
sections.title: Outsourcing non-critical functions: access and audit rights
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure access and audit rights for non-critical functions as specified in paragraph 87. They should assess the nature of the outsourced function, operational and reputational risks, scalability, impact on continuous operations, and contractual duration to determine criticality over time.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to verify that non-critical functions maintain appropriate access and audit rights, ensuring ongoing compliance without assuming criticality.

requirements.text: Ensure access and audit rights as set out in paragraph 87 (a) and (b)

sections.source_quote: 88. For the outsourcing of functions that are not critical or important, institutions and payment institutions should ensure the access and audit rights as set out in paragraph 87 (a) and (b) and
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing access control critical for data compliance

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the outsourcing agreement or any
sections.title: Outsourcing agreements must not impede access and audit rights
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure that outsourcing agreements or contractual arrangements do not restrict their ability to exercise access and audit rights, as well as those of competent authorities or third parties appointed to exercise these rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing contracts must not limit access and audit rights, ensuring regulatory oversight and data security for critical functions.

requirements.text: Outsourcing agreement or any other contractual arrangement must not impede or limit effective exercise of access and audit rights by them, competent authorities or third parties appointed by them

sections.source_quote: 89. Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that the outsourcing agreement or any other contractual arrangement does not impede or limit the effective exercise of the access and audit rights by them, competent authorities or third parties appointed by them to exercise these rights.
sections.relevance_reason: Outsourcing access and audit rights

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should exercise their access and audit rights, determine
sections.title: Risk-based audit requirements for outsourced functions
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must conduct risk-based audits for outsourced functions, determining audit frequency and scope while adhering to relevant national and international audit standards to ensure effective oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires risk-based audits for outsourced functions to maintain data security and compliance without overburdening resources.

requirements.text: Exercise access and audit rights, determine audit frequency and areas to be audited on a risk-based approach
Adhere to relevant, commonly accepted, national and international audit standards

sections.source_quote: 90. Institutions and payment institutions should exercise their access and audit rights, determine the audit frequency and areas to be audited on a risk-based approach and adhere to relevant, commonly accepted, national and international audit standards.
sections.relevance_reason: Risk-based audits for outsourced data functions

sections.heading: Without prejudice to their final responsibility regarding outsourcing arrangements, institutions
sections.title: Use of pooled audits and third-party certifications
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions may use pooled audits with other clients to share audit resources and third-party certifications or audit reports to reduce organizational burden, provided they meet specific conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows institutions to use pooled audits and third-party certifications but requires verification of their adequacy for critical functions.

requirements.text: Use pooled audits organised jointly with other clients of the same service provider, performed by them and these clients or by a third party appointed by them
Use third-party certifications and third-party or internal audit reports made available by the service provider

sections.source_quote: 91. Institutions and payment institutions may use: a. pooled audits organised jointly with other clients of the same service provider, and performed by them and these clients or by a third party appointed by them, to use audit resources more efficiently and to decrease the organisational burden on both the clients and the service provider; b. third-party certifications and third-party or internal audit reports, made available by the service provider.
sections.relevance_reason: outsourcing third-party audits

sections.heading: For the outsourcing of critical or important functions, institutions and payment institutions
sections.title: Critical functions: verification of third-party certifications
sections.summary: For critical or important functions, institutions must verify that third-party certifications and reports are adequate to comply with regulatory obligations and not rely solely on them over time.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to validate third-party certifications for critical functions to ensure ongoing compliance and data security.

requirements.text: Assess whether third-party certifications and reports are adequate and sufficient to comply with regulatory obligations
Not rely solely on these reports over time

sections.source_quote: 92. For the outsourcing of critical or important functions, institutions and payment institutions should assess whether third-party certifications and reports as referred to in paragraph 91(b) are adequate and sufficient to comply with their regulatory obligations and should not rely solely on these reports over time.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical functions outsourcing verification

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should make use of the method referred to in
sections.title: Conditions for using third-party certifications in critical functions
sections.summary: Institutions must satisfy specific conditions when using third-party certifications for critical functions, including audit plan satisfaction, scope coverage, ongoing assessment, and contractual rights to expand audits.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets detailed conditions for using third-party certifications in critical functions to ensure robust oversight and compliance.

requirements.text: Be satisfied with the audit plan for the outsourced function
Ensure the scope of the certification or audit report covers systems and key controls identified by the institution
Thoroughly assess the content of certifications or audit reports on an ongoing basis
Verify that certifications or reports are not obsolete
Ensure key systems and controls are covered in future versions of certifications or audit reports
Be satisfied with the aptitude of the certifying or auditing party
Ensure certifications are issued and audits performed against widely recognised professional standards
Have contractual right to request expansion of scope of certifications or audit reports
Retain contractual right to perform individual audits at discretion

sections.source_quote: 93. Institutions and payment institutions should make use of the method referred to in paragraph 91(b) only if they: a. are satisfied with the audit plan for the outsourced function; b. ensure that the scope of the certification or audit report covers the systems (i.e. processes, applications, infrastructure, data centres, etc.) and key controls identified by the institution or payment institution and the compliance with relevant regulatory requirements; c. thoroughly assess the content of the certifications or audit reports on an ongoing basis and verify that the reports or certifications are not obsolete; d. ensure that key systems and controls are covered in future versions of the certification or audit report; e. are satisfied with the aptitude of the certifying or auditing party (e.g. with regard to rotation of the certifying or auditing company, qualifications, expertise, re-performance/verification of the evidence in the underlying audit file); f. are satisfied that the certifications are issued and the audits are performed against widely recognised relevant professional standards and include a test of the operational effectiveness of the key controls in place; g. have the contractual right to request the expansion of the scope of the certifications or audit reports to other relevant systems and controls; the number and frequency of such requests for scope modification should be reasonable and legitimate from a risk management perspective; and h. retain the contractual right to perform individual audits at their discretion with regard to the outsourcing of critical or important functions.
sections.relevance_reason: Third-party certification for critical functions in outsourcing

sections.heading: In line with the EBA Guidelines on ICT risk assessment under the SREP, institutions should,
sections.title: Security penetration testing for ICT security measures
sections.summary: Institutions must conduct security penetration testing to evaluate cyber and internal ICT security measures and processes. Payment institutions additionally require internal ICT control mechanisms with specific security controls and mitigation measures as part of their risk assessment under the SREP framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires security testing and internal controls for ICT systems, relevant for assessing data transfer security in outsourcing arrangements.

requirements.text: Institutions should ensure they can carry out security penetration testing to assess cyber and internal ICT security measures and processes
Payment institutions should have internal ICT control mechanisms including ICT security control and mitigation measures

sections.source_quote: 94. In line with the EBA Guidelines on ICT risk assessment under the SREP, institutions should, where relevant, ensure that they are able to carry out security penetration testing to assess the effectiveness of implemented cyber and internal ICT security measures and processes.
sections.relevance_reason: Core security testing for data protection

sections.heading: Before a planned on-site visit, institutions, payment institutions, competent authorities and
sections.title: Notice requirements for on-site audits
sections.summary: Institutions, payment institutions, competent authorities, and auditors must provide reasonable notice to service providers before on-site visits unless emergencies or crises prevent it, or the audit would become ineffective.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires advance notice for audits, impacting third-party data access and audit effectiveness in cross-border outsourcing scenarios.

requirements.text: Institutions, payment institutions, competent authorities and auditors should provide reasonable notice to the service provider before on-site visits

sections.source_quote: 95. Before a planned on-site visit, institutions, payment institutions, competent authorities and auditors or third parties acting on behalf of the institution, payment institution or competent authorities should provide reasonable notice to the service provider, unless this is not possible due to an emergency or crisis situation or would lead to a situation where the audit would no longer be effective.
sections.relevance_reason: on-site audits require notice for data access

sections.heading: When performing audits in multi-client environments, care should be taken to ensure that risks
sections.title: Risk mitigation in multi-client audits
sections.summary: When conducting audits in multi-client environments, institutions must avoid or mitigate risks to other clients' data availability, service levels, and confidentiality to prevent cross-contamination.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses cross-client data risks during audits, critical for ensuring data isolation in shared infrastructure scenarios.

requirements.text: Care should be taken to ensure risks to another client's environment (e.g. impact on service levels, availability of data, confidentiality aspects) are avoided or mitigated

sections.source_quote: 96. When performing audits in multi-client environments, care should be taken to ensure that risks to another client's environment (e.g. impact on service levels, availability of data, confidentiality aspects) are avoided or mitigated.
sections.relevance_reason: Risk mitigation for cross-client data security

sections.heading: Where the outsourcing arrangement carries a high level of technical complexity, for instance in
sections.title: Auditor competency verification for complex outsourcing
sections.summary: For technically complex outsourcing arrangements like cloud services, institutions must verify that auditors have appropriate skills and knowledge to conduct effective audits and assessments of the service provider.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures competent auditors for complex outsourcing, vital for assessing data security in cloud environments.

requirements.text: Institutions should verify that auditors have appropriate and relevant skills and knowledge to perform relevant audits and assessments effectively
Institutions should verify that staff reviewing third-party certifications have appropriate skills and knowledge

sections.source_quote: 97. Where the outsourcing arrangement carries a high level of technical complexity, for instance in the case of cloud outsourcing, the institution or payment institution should verify that whoever is performing the audit – whether it is its internal auditors, the pool of auditors or external auditors acting on its behalf – has appropriate and relevant skills and knowledge to perform relevant audits and/or assessments effectively.
sections.relevance_reason: Auditor competency for outsourcing security

sections.heading: The outsourcing arrangement should expressly allow the possibility for the institution or
sections.title: Termination rights in outsourcing arrangements
sections.summary: Outsourcing arrangements must allow termination under specific conditions including legal breaches, performance issues, material changes, data security weaknesses, and regulatory directives from competent authorities.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines termination triggers for outsourcing, critical for data transfer continuity and regulatory compliance in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Outsourcing arrangements should allow termination where the provider breaches applicable law, regulations or contractual provisions
Outsourcing arrangements should allow termination where impediments capable of altering the performance of outsourced functions are identified
Outsourcing arrangements should allow termination where material changes affect the outsourcing arrangement or service provider
Outsourcing arrangements should allow termination where there are weaknesses in the management and security of confidential, personal or sensitive data
Outsourcing arrangements should allow termination where competent authorities instruct termination due to ineffective supervision

sections.source_quote: 98. The outsourcing arrangement should expressly allow the possibility for the institution or payment institution to terminate the arrangement, in accordance with applicable law, including in the following situations: a. where the provider of the outsourced functions is in a breach of applicable law, regulations or contractual provisions; b. where impediments capable of altering the performance of the outsourced function are identified; c. where there are material changes affecting the outsourcing arrangement or the service provider (e.g. sub-outsourcing or changes of sub-contractors); d. where there are weaknesses regarding the management and security of confidential, personal or otherwise sensitive data or information; and e. where instructions are given by the institution's or payment institution's competent authority, e.g. in the case that the competent authority is, caused by the outsourcing arrangement, no longer in a position to effectively supervise the institution or payment institution.
sections.relevance_reason: Termination rights for data security breaches and outsourcing

sections.heading: The outsourcing arrangement should facilitate the transfer of the outsourced function to
sections.title: Function transfer provisions in outsourcing
sections.summary: Outsourcing agreements must facilitate smooth transfer of outsourced functions to another provider or back to the institution, including data treatment obligations, transition periods, and support for orderly transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures seamless function transfers during outsourcing termination, critical for maintaining data continuity in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Written outsourcing arrangements should clearly set out obligations of existing service providers during function transfers
Written outsourcing arrangements should set an appropriate transition period after termination
Written outsourcing arrangements should include service provider obligations to support orderly function transfers

sections.source_quote: 99. The outsourcing arrangement should facilitate the transfer of the outsourced function to another service provider or its re-incorporation into the institution or payment institution. To this end, the written outsourcing arrangement should: a. clearly set out the obligations of the existing service provider, in the case of a transfer of the outsourced function to another service provider or back to the institution or payment institution, including the treatment of data; b. set an appropriate transition period, during which the service provider, after the termination of the outsourcing arrangement, would continue to provide the outsourced function to reduce the risk of disruptions; and c. include an obligation of the service provider to support the institution or payment institution in the orderly transfer of the function in the event of the termination of the outsourcing agreement.
sections.relevance_reason: Core outsourcing transfer obligations for GDPR

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should monitor, on an ongoing basis, the
sections.title: Ongoing monitoring of outsourced service performance
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must continuously monitor service providers' performance for outsourcing arrangements using a risk-based approach, prioritizing critical functions. This monitoring ensures data availability, integrity, and security. Institutions must reassess the criticality of outsourced functions if risks, nature, or scale change significantly.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires ongoing risk-based monitoring of outsourced functions to ensure data security and integrity, critical for DTIA when transferring data via third parties.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should monitor service providers' performance on an ongoing basis for outsourcing arrangements using a risk-based approach
Institutions and payment institutions should focus monitoring on outsourced critical or important functions
Institutions and payment institutions must ensure data availability, integrity, and security
Institutions must reassess the criticality of outsourced functions if risk, nature, or scale changes materially

sections.source_quote: Institutions and payment institutions should monitor, on an ongoing basis, the performance of the service providers with regard to all outsourcing arrangements on a risk-based approach and with the main focus being on the outsourcing of critical or important functions, including that the availability, integrity and security of data and information is ensured. Where the risk, nature or scale of an outsourced function has materially changed, institutions and payment institutions should reassess the criticality or importance of that function in line with Section 4.
sections.relevance_reason: core outsourcing monitoring for data security

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should apply due skill, care and diligence when
sections.title: Application of due skill and care in outsourcing management
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must apply due skill, care, and diligence when managing outsourcing arrangements. This includes monitoring and overseeing service providers to ensure compliance with legal and regulatory standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates due diligence in outsourcing to prevent data breaches and ensure compliance, critical for DTIA when handling sensitive data across borders.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should apply due skill, care and diligence when monitoring and managing outsourcing arrangements

sections.source_quote: Institutions and payment institutions should apply due skill, care and diligence when monitoring and managing outsourcing arrangements.
sections.relevance_reason: outsource compliance critical for data protection

sections.heading: Institutions should regularly update their risk assessment in accordance with
sections.title: Regular risk assessment updates for outsourcing
sections.summary: Institutions must regularly update their risk assessments for outsourcing arrangements, particularly for critical or important functions. They must also periodically report identified risks to their management bodies to ensure oversight and accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular risk assessment updates to address evolving threats, essential for DTIA when outsourcing data processing across borders.

requirements.text: Institutions should regularly update their risk assessment in accordance with Section 12.2
Institutions should periodically report to the management body on risks identified in outsourcing critical or important functions

sections.source_quote: Institutions should regularly update their risk assessment in accordance with Section 12.2 and should periodically report to the management body on the risks identified in respect of the outsourcing of critical or important functions.
sections.relevance_reason: outsource risk assessment updates for critical functions

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should monitor and manage their internal
sections.title: Management of internal concentration risks from outsourcing
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must monitor and manage internal concentration risks caused by outsourcing arrangements, particularly for critical or important functions. This ensures that outsourced activities do not create undue vulnerabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses concentration risks from outsourcing, critical for DTIA when data is processed by multiple third parties across borders.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should monitor and manage internal concentration risks caused by outsourcing arrangements

sections.source_quote: Institutions and payment institutions should monitor and manage their internal concentration risks caused by outsourcing arrangements, taking into account Section 12.2 of these guidelines.
sections.relevance_reason: outsource risk management for critical functions

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure, on an ongoing basis, that outsourcing
sections.title: Ongoing performance standards for outsourced functions
sections.summary: Institutions must ensure outsourced functions meet performance and quality standards through regular reporting, performance evaluations, and reviews of service providers' compliance with policies and legal requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures outsourced functions meet quality standards, critical for DTIA when transferring data to third parties across borders.

requirements.text: Institutions should ensure outsourced arrangements meet performance and quality standards through reports from service providers
Institutions should evaluate service provider performance using key performance indicators, key control indicators, service delivery reports, self-certification, and independent reviews
Institutions should review all relevant information from service providers, including business continuity measures and testing

sections.source_quote: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure, on an ongoing basis, that outsourcing arrangements, with the main focus being on outsourced critical or important functions, meet appropriate performance and quality standards in line with their policies by: a. ensuring that they receive appropriate reports from service providers; b. evaluating the performance of service providers using tools such as key performance indicators, key control indicators, service delivery reports, self-certification and independent reviews; and c. reviewing all other relevant information received from the service provider, including reports on business continuity measures and testing.
sections.relevance_reason: outsourcing compliance for data protection

sections.heading: Institutions should take appropriate measures if they identify shortcomings in the provision
sections.title: Corrective actions for outsourced function shortcomings
sections.summary: Institutions must take corrective actions if they identify shortcomings in outsourced functions, including terminating agreements immediately if service providers fail to comply with legal or regulatory requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires immediate corrective actions for outsourced function failures, critical for DTIA to prevent data breaches and ensure compliance.

requirements.text: Institutions should take appropriate measures if they identify shortcomings in the provision of outsourced functions
Institutions should follow up on indications that service providers may not be carrying out outsourced functions effectively or in compliance with laws and regulations
Institutions should take corrective or remedial actions if shortcomings are identified
Institutions may terminate outsourcing agreements with immediate effect if necessary

sections.source_quote: Institutions should take appropriate measures if they identify shortcomings in the provision of the outsourced function. In particular, institutions and payment institutions should follow up on any indications that service providers may not be carrying out the outsourced critical or important function effectively or in compliance with applicable laws and regulatory requirements. If shortcomings are identified, institutions and payment institutions should take appropriate corrective or remedial actions. Such actions may include terminating the outsourcing agreement, with immediate effect, if necessary.
sections.relevance_reason: Corrective actions for outsourcing compliance

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should have a documented exit strategy when
sections.title: Documented exit strategy for critical outsourced functions
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must maintain documented exit strategies for outsourcing critical or important functions. These strategies must align with their outsourcing policies and business continuity plans. The exit strategy must address potential scenarios including termination of arrangements, service provider failure, quality deterioration, and material risks to function continuity.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires documented exit strategies for critical outsourced functions to ensure business continuity during transitions, relevant for DTIA when assessing outsourcing risks across borders.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should have a documented exit strategy for outsourcing critical or important functions
The exit strategy must be in line with outsourcing policy and business continuity plans
The exit strategy must consider at least the possibility of termination of outsourcing arrangements
The exit strategy must consider the possibility of service provider failure
The exit strategy must consider deterioration of function quality and business disruptions
The exit strategy must consider material risks to continuous function application

sections.source_quote: 106. Institutions and payment institutions should have a documented exit strategy when outsourcing critical or important functions that is in line with their outsourcing policy and business continuity plans, taking into account at least the possibility of: (a) the termination of outsourcing arrangements; (b) the failure of the service provider; (c) the deterioration of the quality of the function provided and actual or potential business disruptions caused by the inappropriate or failed provision of the function; (d) material risks arising for the appropriate and continuous application of the function.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical for outsourcing and data continuity under GDPR

sections.heading: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that they are able to exit outsourcing
sections.title: Business continuity during outsourcing exit
sections.summary: Institutions and payment institutions must ensure they can exit outsourcing arrangements without disrupting business, compromising regulatory compliance, or affecting service quality. This requires comprehensive, documented, and tested exit plans that identify alternative solutions and transition mechanisms for moving functions and data to alternative providers or back to the institution.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures business continuity during outsourcing transitions, critical for DTIA when assessing cross-border data transfers and service continuity.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should ensure they can exit outsourcing arrangements without undue disruption to business activities
Institutions and payment institutions should ensure they can exit outsourcing arrangements without limiting compliance with regulatory requirements
Institutions and payment institutions should ensure they can exit outsourcing arrangements without detriment to service continuity and quality
Institutions should develop and implement comprehensive, documented, and sufficiently tested exit plans
Institutions should identify alternative solutions and develop transition plans to remove outsourced functions and data from service providers
Institutions should transfer outsourced functions and data to alternative providers or back to the institution
Institutions should ensure continuous provision of critical functions during transition

sections.source_quote: 107. Institutions and payment institutions should ensure that they are able to exit outsourcing arrangements without undue disruption to their business activities, without limiting their compliance with regulatory requirements and without any detriment to the continuity and quality of its provision of services to clients.
sections.relevance_reason: Directly addresses outsourcing exit plans for data continuity and compliance

sections.heading: When developing exit strategies, institutions and payment institutions should:
sections.title: Exit strategy development requirements
sections.summary: When developing exit strategies, institutions must define objectives, conduct business impact analyses commensurate with risk levels, assign roles and resources, define success criteria for transitions, and establish monitoring indicators including those for unacceptable service levels that trigger exits.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides specific steps for developing exit strategies, relevant for DTIA when assessing the robustness of cross-border data transfer mechanisms.

requirements.text: Institutions and payment institutions should define the objectives of the exit strategy
Institutions should perform business impact analysis commensurate with outsourcing risk levels
Institutions should assign roles, responsibilities and sufficient resources to manage exit plans
Institutions should define success criteria for the transition of outsourced functions and data
Institutions should define indicators for monitoring outsourcing arrangements, including those for unacceptable service levels that trigger exits

sections.source_quote: 108. When developing exit strategies, institutions and payment institutions should: (a) define the objectives of the exit strategy; (b) perform a business impact analysis that is commensurate with the risk of the outsourced processes, services or activities, with the aim of identifying what human and financial resources would be required to implement the exit plan and how much time it would take; (c) assign roles, responsibilities and sufficient resources to manage exit plans and the transition of activities; (d) define success criteria for the transition of outsourced functions and data; and (e) define the indicators to be used for the monitoring of the outsourcing arrangement (as outlined under Section 14), including indicators based on unacceptable service levels that should trigger the exit.
sections.relevance_reason: Exit strategy for outsourcing data transfers

sections.heading: When establishing appropriate methods to monitor institutions' and payment institutions'
sections.title: Monitoring outsourcing compliance with authorisation
sections.summary: Competent authorities must verify that outsourcing arrangements do not constitute material changes to institutions' initial regulatory authorisation conditions. This involves assessing whether outsourcing impacts compliance with existing obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures that outsourcing arrangements do not undermine regulatory compliance, relevant for DTIA when assessing cross-border data transfers and service continuity.

requirements.text: Competent authorities should identify if outsourcing arrangements amount to material changes to initial authorisation conditions

sections.source_quote: 109. When establishing appropriate methods to monitor institutions' and payment institutions' compliance with the conditions for initial authorisation, competent authorities should aim to identify if outsourcing arrangements amount to a material change to the conditions and obligations of institutions' and payment institutions' initial authorisation.
sections.relevance_reason: Competent authorities verify outsourcing compliance with authorisation

sections.heading: Competent authorities should be satisfied that they can effectively supervise institutions
sections.title: Audit rights for supervisory oversight
sections.summary: Competent authorities must ensure that service providers in outsourcing arrangements grant audit and access rights to the authority and institutions, enabling effective regulatory oversight of critical functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures regulatory oversight of outsourced functions, critical for DTIA when assessing data transfer mechanisms and service continuity.

requirements.text: Service providers must grant audit and access rights to competent authorities and institutions

sections.source_quote: 110. Competent authorities should be satisfied that they can effectively supervise institutions and payment institutions, including that institutions or payment institutions have ensured within their outsourcing arrangement that service providers are obliged to grant audit and access rights to the competent authority and the institution, in line with Section 13.3.
sections.relevance_reason: Audit rights for regulatory oversight of outsourcing

sections.heading: The analysis of institutions' outsourcing risks should be performed at least within the SREP
sections.title: Outsourcing risk analysis within SREP
sections.summary: Institutions must conduct outsourcing risk analyses at least within the Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP) or through other supervisory mechanisms, including on-site inspections, to ensure ongoing compliance with regulatory requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures regular assessment of outsourcing risks, relevant for DTIA when evaluating cross-border data transfer mechanisms.

requirements.text: Institutions should perform outsourcing risk analysis at least within the SREP
For payment institutions, outsourcing risk analysis should be conducted as part of other supervisory processes

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities may ask institutions and payment institutions for additional information, in particular for critical or important outsourcing arrangements, such as: a. the detailed risk analysis; b. whether the service provider has a business continuity plan that is suitable for the services provided to the outsourcing institution or payment institution; c. the exit strategy for use if the outsourcing arrangement is terminated by either party or if there is disruption to the provision of the services; and d. the resources and measures in place to adequately monitor the outsourced activities.

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities should assess the following on a risk-based approach: a. whether institutions and payment institutions monitor and manage appropriately, in particular, critical or important outsourcing arrangements; b. whether institutions and payment institutions have sufficient resources in place to monitor and manage outsourcing arrangements; c. whether institutions and payment institutions identify and manage all relevant risks; and d. whether institutions and payment institutions identify, assess and appropriately manage conflicts of interest with regard to outsourcing arrangements, e.g. in the case of intragroup outsourcing or outsourcing within the same institutional protection scheme.

sections.title: Competent authorities should assess outsourcing risks

Competent authorities should take into account reputational risks

sections.source_quote: Within their assessment, competent authorities should take into account all risks, in particular: a. the operational risks posed by the outsourcing arrangement; b. reputational risks; c. the step-in risk that could require the institution to bail out a service provider, in the case of significant institutions; d. concentration risks within the institution, including on a consolidated basis, caused by multiple outsourcing arrangements with a single service provider or closely connected service providers or multiple outsourcing arrangements within the same business area; e. concentration risks at the sector level, e.g. where multiple institutions or payment institutions make use of a single service provider or a small group of service providers; f. the extent to which the outsourcing institution or payment institution controls the service provider or has the ability to influence its actions, the reduction of risks that may result from a higher level of control and if the service provider is included in the consolidated supervision of the group; and g. conflicts of interest between the institution and the service provider.

sections.title: Outsourcing arrangements for financial institutions
sections.summary: This section clarifies the application of outsourcing requirements for financial institutions, particularly distinguishing between critical/important functions and non-critical arrangements. It specifies that investment management companies under UCITS or AIFMD directives are excluded from individual-level application but covered under prudential consolidation. The guidelines have been revised to address concerns about proportionality, transitional arrangements, and the scope of outsourcing contracts. This section clarifies that cloud services are not automatically considered outsourcing arrangements under the CRD. It mandates contractual audit rights for critical functions on a risk-based approach, ensuring oversight for cloud and third-party services. The requirements apply to financial institutions within a group structure.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by requiring institutions to assess outsourcing arrangements as critical or important, with specific rules for sub-outsourcing, cloud services, and contractual documentation. It mandates robust governance for critical functions and transitional arrangements for existing contracts. Contractual audit rights for critical functions are required for cloud and third-party services, impacting data transfer and storage assessments for financial institutions using outsourced IT solutions.

If the service provider is in a third country, additional requirements apply. Functions not requiring licensing can be outsourced to any provider as long as institutional authorisation conditions are maintained. Institutions must conduct due diligence to verify service provider authorisation or registration status, including for intragroup outsourcing.

Third-country providers require additional compliance with data protection and supervisory cooperation agreements. Institutions must verify provider authorisation status through due diligence.

sections.title: Outsourcing audit rights and risk-based monitoring for critical functions
sections.summary: This section clarifies that institutions must implement risk-based audit rights for critical or important outsourced functions, ensuring third-party providers meet governance standards. It mandates that institutions conduct regular reviews of outsourced activities using a three-lines-of-defence approach, with the third line responsible for audit functions. The guidelines specify that institutions should not rely solely on service providers' internal audit functions for ongoing monitoring, particularly for high-risk functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires institutions to assess and monitor outsourced functions with criticality, ensuring audit rights are maintained for high-risk activities to prevent data breaches and service disruptions during third-party provider failures.

sections.title: Clarification of outsourcing conditions and critical functions
sections.summary: This section clarifies that the guidelines for outsourcing arrangements should be merged with other provisions. It addresses the meaning of 'conduct' in paragraph 51(b)(iii), noting it relates to institutions' conduct towards customers, employees, or other stakeholders. The guidelines align with MiFID II's definition of critical or important functions for outsourcing, providing additional criteria for assessing function criticality. Institutions must apply proportionality when evaluating outsourcing arrangements.

sections.title: Sub-outsourcing controls and approval requirements for financial institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions may relax sub-outsourcing controls when exiting agreements without cost. Prior authorisation for sub-outsourcing can be provided in general terms rather than per case. Institutions must ensure sub-contractors do not directly engage with them and must monitor risks where sub-outsourcing occurs. Institutions retain final responsibility for critical functions and can terminate contracts if sub-outsourcing is not approved or objected to.
sections.dtia_summary: Sub-outsourcing arrangements require institutions to define approval scope, monitor sub-contractors, and ensure termination rights when sub-outsourcing is not approved. This affects cross-border data transfers involving third parties.

sections.title: Outsourcing arrangements: register requirements and exemptions
sections.summary: The guidelines specify that institutions must identify alternative solutions but not maintain alternate arrangements. They may include service provider changes in business continuity plans. Institutions can define ex ante performance indicators for service levels. Payment and electronic money institutions are exempt from full register requirements but must document outsourcing arrangements. Intragroup arrangements may be excluded from information requirements where they lack added value. The register has been streamlined for non-material outsourcing arrangements.
